title: Backprop Through Activation Functions
concept: Activation functions do more than shape forward predictions; during backpropagation, their slope controls how much of the error signal flows backward through a neuron.

exercises.prompt: Recap: After the loss is computed, which layer receives the first backward feedback?
exercises.explain: Right! The output layer is the first stop in the backward pass.

cards.title: A door that can open wide, barely crack, or slam shut
cards.body: Imagine trying to pass a note backward through classroom doors. Some doors swing open easily. Some barely let the note through. Some are stuck shut. Activation functions act like those doors during backpropagation.

cards.title: Slope decides the gradient flow
cards.body: During backpropagation, an activation function's slope acts like a gate. A steeper, open region lets more of the error signal pass through. A flat or blocked region lets little through — or none at all.

cards.title: Water through different valves
cards.body: Think of the backward signal like water in a hose. A wide-open valve lets the water rush through. A half-closed valve weakens the flow. A closed valve blocks it completely. Backprop through activations works the same way.

cards.title: Sigmoid can weaken, ReLU can block
cards.body: If a Sigmoid neuron is already saturated near 0 or 1, its slope is tiny, so the backward signal becomes weak. If a ReLU neuron was negative and output 0, it can block the signal completely. But a ReLU neuron in its positive region usually lets the signal pass cleanly.

cards.title: Activations affect learning, not just prediction
cards.body: It is tempting to think activation functions matter only during the forward pass because they shape outputs. But they also shape learning itself by deciding how strongly gradients move backward through the network.

cards.title: Two gates, two different backward flows
cards.body: How common activations influence the backward signal:

left.text:
Neuron output was above 0.
Gate is open.
Backward signal usually passes through strongly.

right.text:
Neuron output is near 0 or 1.
Gate gets narrow.
Backward signal becomes weaker.

exercises.prompt: What does an activation function's slope do during backpropagation?

options.text: It controls how much of the error signal is allowed to flow backward through the neuron.
It chooses a new label for the dataset.
It stores the model permanently on disk.
It removes the need for a loss function.

exercises.explain: Correct! The slope acts like a gate for the backward signal.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A ReLU neuron that output 0 because its input was negative can completely block the backward signal.
exercises.explain: True! In its inactive region, ReLU can stop the gradient from passing backward.

exercises.sentence: During backpropagation, an activation function behaves like a gradient ___.
exercises.explain: A gate is the right idea: activations decide how much backward signal gets through.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario describes a weak gradient caused by an activation function?

options.text: A Sigmoid output is already pinned near 1, so the backward signal passing through that neuron becomes very small.
The training set is split into validation and test sets.
A model stores its final accuracy in a spreadsheet.

exercises.explain: Correct! Sigmoid near its extremes can shrink the gradient a lot.

exercises.prompt: Two hidden neurons receive the same backward signal from the layer above. Neuron A used ReLU and was active with a positive output. Neuron B used Sigmoid and was already saturated near 1. Which neuron is more likely to pass a stronger gradient backward, and why?

options.text: Neuron A, because an active ReLU usually leaves the gate open, while a saturated Sigmoid weakens the signal.
Neuron B, because Sigmoid always amplifies the gradient near 1.
Neither; activation functions only matter in the forward pass.
Neuron B, because saturated outputs guarantee faster learning.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Active ReLU often passes gradients cleanly, while a saturated Sigmoid can squeeze them down. Next, we will follow those gradients deeper into hidden layers and see how neurons pass blame backward.
